title: Twisted Kagome Bilayers: Higher-Order Magic Angles, Topological Flat Bands, and Sublattice Interference
authors: David T. S. Perkins, Joseph J. Betouras
abstract: We develop a low-energy continuum model to describe the moiré physics of heterostructures, which is a generalization of the celebrated Bistritzer-MacDonald (BM) method [R. Bistritzer and A. H. MacDonald, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 108, 12233 (2011)]. We take as an example the moiré physics of electrons in twisted bilayer kagomé (TBK) metals near $1/3$ filling where monolayer Dirac cones lie. We demonstrate the emergence of higher-order magic angles where significant local band flattening occurs as a high-order Van Hove singularity emerges and show how twisting alone can induce non-trivial topology. We, furthermore, show that while sublattice interference effects are present, their role is not as prominent as in monolayer kagome.
summary: This paper presents a theoretical framework for understanding twisted kagome bilayers, identifying specific 'higher-order magic angles' where electronic bands become extremely flat. It reveals that twisting these layers can induce non-trivial topological phases and higher-order Van Hove singularities, offering a new platform for studying correlated physics.
main_problem: Investigating the emergence of higher-order magic angles, topological flat bands, and the role of sublattice interference in twisted kagome bilayers (TBK).
main_result: The study demonstrates the emergence of higher-order magic angles where significant band flattening and higher-order Van Hove singularities occur, and shows that twisting can induce non-trivial topology.
method: A generalized Bistritzer-MacDonald continuum model is developed by projecting the kagome monolayer Hamiltonian onto the Dirac subspace and truncating the tunneling Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The system is a twisted kagome bilayer (TBK) heterostructure near 1/3 filling, modeled using a low-energy continuum Hamiltonian that incorporates interlayer tunneling and $p_z$-orbital-based parameters.
key_observables: Fermi velocity (vF), band flattening, Van Hove singularities (VHS), higher-order Van Hove singularities (HOVHS), and valley Chern numbers.
important_parameters: Twist angle (theta), interlayer tunneling energy scale (omega_0), layer separation (d_perp), and 1/3 filling regime.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the physics is dominated by the Dirac cones (neglecting higher-energy bands), uses a truncated tunneling Hamiltonian, and assumes rapid decay of interlayer tunneling with momentum.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows kagome geometry and MBZ construction; Fig 2 displays band structures, contour plots, and velocity renormalization; Fig 3 shows layer-resolved sublattice projections.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the TBK system and geometry, develops a generalized continuum model, analyzes the emergence of magic angles and higher-order singularities, investigates topological properties and symmetry breaking, and discusses the role of sublattice interference.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a): Monolayer kagome lattice with the sublattice, lattice vectors, and nearest-neighbor distance labelled. The yellow dot corresponds to the unit cell origin from which the sublattice positions, {δi}, are defined and the centre of ro- tation used to define the twisted bilayer. (b): Construction of the TBK system in the twist symmetric frame (¯ϑ = −ϑ), yielding a total twist of 2ϑ = θ. (c): Definition of the MBZ (shaded region) by connection of two Dirac points belonging to the BZs of the neighboring layers. (d): Labelling of the MBZ high-symmetry points and the high-symmetry path.

FIG. 2. (a): The band structure of TBK near 1/3 filling for various twist angles with the energy zeroed average energy of the first conduction band (solid blue line) and first valence band at K′ M. Solid (dashed) lines indicate the monolayer valley τ = +1 (τ = −1), while n ̸= 0 labels the band number. We highlight the non-abelian valley Chern number of the first conduction and valence bands for θ = 0.95◦and the abelian valley Chern number for n = −3 with θ = 0.7◦. (b): Contour plots of the first conduction (left) and valence (right) bands for θ = 0.95◦. Dashed lines mark the MBZ boundary. Shaded regions indicate the high-symmetry point of interest in panel (e). (c): Band structure for θ =...
FIG. 3. Layer-resolved sublattice projection of the first con- duction (top) and valence (bottom) bands for the θ = 0.95◦
